concessions.ask: Hold card take rate at 0.85% (+ SLA)

plan_b_scenarios.ai_analysis: This is the highest-probability failure mode. Carlos was hired 6 months ago with an explicit cost-reduction mandate from the board post-Series A. His rejection will not be about price — it will be a signal that his credibility at FlexiPay depends on visible cost wins. The counter must give him a win without ceding the strategic frame.
response_plan: Escalate to Yuno CEO → Sofia Alvarez within 48 hours. Frame as strategic partnership, not vendor negotiation.
Offer Carlos a 'CFO Dashboard' with monthly performance SLA reporting — gives him visible accountability vs dLocal's opacity.
Introduce a tiered pricing structure: 0.85% on first $15M/mo, 0.78% on TPV above — ties Yuno's discount to growth Carlos wants anyway.
Deliver a written risk memo to FlexiPay's board (with Sofia's permission) on dLocal commitment trap and tokenization migration risk.

plan_b_scenarios.trigger: 90-day approval-rate sprint misses targets in Peru
plan_b_scenarios.ai_analysis: Peru is structurally hard: 2 acquirers, small issuer base, limited routing optionality. If we miss PE targets, the SLA exposes Yuno financially AND gives dLocal a 'told you so' narrative. The key is to set PE targets conservatively upfront AND have a contingency acquirer integration ready.
response_plan: Pre-negotiate a 3rd Peru acquirer integration (Niubiz or Izipay) ready to deploy within 30 days of first SLA miss.
Structure the SLA with market-specific floors: MX 82%, CO 80%, CL 80%, PE 75% — not a single blended number.
Add a 'rolling 90-day' measurement window to avoid single-month noise triggering SLA credits.
Set aside $50K reserve for PE-specific acquirer onboarding fees in the Q2 budget.

plan_b_scenarios.trigger: dLocal drops card take rate to 0.60% or lower
plan_b_scenarios.ai_analysis: This is the 'race to the bottom' scenario. If dLocal drops to 0.60%, they are buying the account at a loss — which tells you everything about how strategically they see FlexiPay. Do NOT match. Matching resets Yuno's entire LatAm pricing floor for every other customer. Instead, compete on structural differentiation.
response_plan: Do NOT drop below 0.80% on cards. This is a hard floor — message from VP to sales team.
Shift the narrative entirely to performance-based commercial terms: 'pay for outcomes, not volume'.
Introduce a success-fee structure: 0.85% base + rebate of 5 bps per percentage point of approval rate improvement above a 82% floor. Aligns incentives, quantifies the value.

Expose dLocal's unit economics publicly: if they're selling at 0.60%, they're losing money on FlexiPay, which means service quality will erode within 12 months. Get this into Daniela's hands as internal intel.

plan_b_scenarios.trigger: FlexiPay delays renewal decision past 45-day window
plan_b_scenarios.ai_analysis: Counter-intuitively, this helps us. Yuno's contract auto-renews unless 60-day notice is given. If FlexiPay misses the window, we get another year by default. The risk is they give notice as a negotiation tactic — so we need to frame urgency without creating a hostile countdown.
response_plan: Quietly confirm internally with Daniela that the 60-day notice window has passed (if current date > T-60 days from March 15 renewal).
Do NOT mention the auto-renew clause proactively — it removes their incentive to close.
Schedule a 'Brazil readiness review' for day 30 that forces a commitment decision (they can't launch BR without vendor certainty).
Prepare to offer a 60-day grace period on renewal signing IF they commit to Brazil fast-track — decouples the two decisions.

plan_b_scenarios.ai_analysis: Low probability but catastrophic. Daniela is the only stakeholder actively defending Yuno. If she leaves, her replacement may be hired BY Carlos with a cost mandate. We need to broaden the champion network now, not reactively.
response_plan: Immediately identify 2nd-tier champions within FlexiPay engineering: the 4 backend devs. They feel the support quality directly.
Schedule a 'Yuno Engineering Open House' — Yuno Solutions Eng + FlexiPay devs, no KAM present. Builds ground-level relationships.
Document every deliverable in writing with Daniela CC'd, so a successor has evidence of Yuno value without Daniela's verbal endorsement.
If Daniela leaves, extend her a Yuno advisor role offer within 48 hours — preserves relationship + signals to market.

plan_b_scenarios.trigger: FlexiPay signs with dLocal — account lost
plan_b_scenarios.ai_analysis: The ultimate failure mode. If it happens, the goal shifts from retention to maximum strategic value extraction: preserve relationship, turn the loss into pipeline intelligence, and create a win-back path.
response_plan: Offer a 6-month 'safety net' clause: 30-day pre-approved path back to Yuno at original pricing if dLocal underperforms. Creates a credible win-back trigger.
Turn FlexiPay's 18-month track record into a public case study (with their permission) — fuels the 20+ BNPL prospects in LatAm pipeline.
Extract detailed post-mortem data from Daniela on WHY they switched — use it to harden the next 5 renewals across the portfolio.
Schedule quarterly check-ins with Daniela personally — she came from Mercado Pago, she'll move again. This is a 5-year relationship, not an 18-month one.
If FlexiPay's BR launch is delayed because of the switch, Yuno uses it publicly (carefully) as evidence that orchestration speed matters. Turn their mistake into pipeline.

qbr_scripts.tone: Bottom-line first. Zero fluff. Spreadsheets and numbers.
qbr_scripts.opening_hook: Carlos, before anything else — the dLocal savings number is actually $427,920, not $370K. I want you to have the right number on the table.
three_bullets: dLocal's $427K headline savings destroy $707K of FlexiPay value in Year 1 once you price in 160 eng hours, 2-month Brazil delay, 2-point approval drop, token migration, and the $4.3M/mo commitment shortfall you're exposed to on their 24-month term.

Your current Yuno spend is 0.83% of TPV — that's the median of the LatAm orchestration market, not the top. The real benchmarking question isn't 'is 0.85% expensive', it's 'what does 0.67% buy you?' — and the answer is: a single-acquirer setup in a market where your Peru routing is already the weakest link.

Here's what I'm authorized to give you: platform fee waived ($24K/yr), PIX matched to 0.50% on a 24-month commit, performance SLA with approval-rate floors, and 18-month term with no hard TPV minimum. Total concession value: ~$75K. Your line-item wins, your Brazil ships on time, and you don't inherit dLocal's commitment trap. Sign it, and Q1 goes home quiet.
qbr_scripts.close: I can have this in writing by end of day tomorrow. What do you need from me to green-light it?

qbr_scripts.tone: Data-driven, technical, hates fluff. Show your work.
qbr_scripts.opening_hook: Daniela, I built the case you asked me for. Here's the one-line version: the switching math doesn't work for FlexiPay at any price, and I can back every number.
three_bullets: Your January TPV dip is seasonal — proportional across all 4 markets, support tickets flat, 54% 6-month trailing growth intact. Don't let Carlos read the dip as a platform problem. It's not.

Your real performance issue is Peru and Colombia routing — you're 4.3 points under benchmark in CO and 7.3 under in PE. That's $6.1M/yr of FlexiPay GMV being declined at checkout and $305K of net revenue you're leaving on the table. I'm committing to a 90-day sprint to close those gaps, with market-specific SLAs backing it.

Brazil goes live in 3 weeks on Yuno vs 6-8 weeks on dLocal. Your 4-dev team doesn't have 1,280 engineering hours to spend re-integrating. Switching costs you the Brazil launch Sofia promised the board — and switching cost itself is $1.13M Year 1 vs $427K headline savings. I need you co-presenting this with me to Carlos.
qbr_scripts.close: Will you co-sign the narrative in tomorrow's QBR? I'll hand you the deck. You hand Carlos the math.

qbr_scripts.tone: Big picture. Competitive positioning. How does this help us win?
qbr_scripts.opening_hook: Sofia, this decision isn't about payments. It's about whether FlexiPay ships Brazil in Q1 or not.
three_bullets: You said 2025 is your Brazil year and first-mover advantage matters. Yuno already supports PIX, Boleto, and local Brazilian acquiring on your existing integration — 3 weeks to live. dLocal needs a full re-integration: 6-8 weeks, minimum. That's 2 months of first-mover advantage you cannot get back. Your competitors ship while you're re-plumbing.

Under our Base case, Brazil + Argentina generate $1.24M of net FlexiPay revenue in Year 1 — 2.9× the dLocal headline savings. The decision isn't 'save $427K on a line item'. It's 'capture $1.24M of new market or don't'. I have the model in front of me.

Yuno is asking for an executive call with you directly — our CEO to you, in the next 14 days. This is positioned as a strategic partnership for FlexiPay's Series B expansion, not a vendor renewal. Your Head of Payments trusts us, your CFO has a cost lens, and you break ties. We want you to break them with the Brazil lens, not the line-item lens.
qbr_scripts.close: Can we put the executive call on the calendar this week?

objection_bank.objection: dLocal saves us $370K a year. That's real money.
objection_bank.short_answer: Actually $427,920. And switching costs $1.13M in Year 1.
objection_bank.full_response: Carlos, let me correct the number first — at your Jan volumes, dLocal's real savings are $427,920, not $370K. I'd rather you work with the right number. Now the harder truth: that $427K evaporates before month 3 once you price in $24K of re-integration engineering, $337K of Brazil launch delay at our Base case, $248K of approval-rate regression from moving to a single-acquirer setup, $180K of tokenization migration churn, and $345K of commitment overage exposure on their $25M/month minimum. Total Year-1 switching cost: $1,135,620. Net impact of switching: negative $707,700. You don't save money. You lose it, and you lose the Brazil launch.

objection_bank.objection: Your 0.85% is above market. I have quotes at 0.65%.
objection_bank.short_answer: 0.85% is the median of LatAm orchestration. 0.65% is single-acquirer pricing.
objection_bank.full_response: 0.85% is the median of the LatAm orchestration market — benchmark range is 0.70% to 0.95% for fintech at your scale. 0.65% is not orchestration pricing — it's single-acquirer pricing, which is a different product. You're comparing a router with 3 acquirers in each market against a pipe to one. If you want to benchmark, benchmark performance: approval rate uplift, latency, fallback behavior. That's where the 0.20% of price delta turns into $248-372K/year of approval-rate value for FlexiPay.

objection_bank.objection: Just match their price on cards. Split the difference at 0.76%.
objection_bank.short_answer: I can give you $75K of concessions that protect your Brazil launch. Not a card rate drop.
objection_bank.full_response: I can't drop the card rate — and I'll tell you why directly. Dropping it to 0.76% gives you $225K/year of pure commercial concession on an account we're already running at commercial discount. It sets our LatAm pricing floor. What I CAN give you is $75K of structured concessions that map to your specific pain: platform fee waived ($24K), PIX matched at 0.50% on a 24-month Brazil commit ($36K), 40 hours of Solutions Engineering on the Brazil fast-track ($15K), 18-month term with no hard TPV minimum, and a performance SLA on approval rates. Same dollar value, more strategic protection, no precedent. Which one do you want?

objection_bank.objection: Our Peru approval rate has been bad for 3 months. Yuno hasn't fixed it.
objection_bank.short_answer: You're right. I'm launching a 90-day sprint with a 3rd Peru acquirer and market-specific SLAs.
objection_bank.full_response: You're right — and that's a fair criticism. Peru is at 72.7% blended approval vs an 80% benchmark. I looked at the data and the problem is routing depth: you have 2 acquirers in Peru vs 3 everywhere else, and the routing rules are from June 2024. I'm launching a 90-day sprint with three concrete actions: first, onboarding a third Peru acquirer — either Niubiz or Izipay — within 30 days. Second, Colombia routing refresh, because you're 4.3 points under benchmark there too. Third, baseline metrics locked today so we can measure improvement. I'm also committing to a written SLA with market-specific floors: Peru at 75% (not 80%, because it's structurally harder), Colombia at 80%, Mexico and Chile at 82%. If we miss, you get service credits. That's how this conversation ends differently than last time.

objection_bank.objection: How do I know Yuno's routing is actually better than dLocal?
objection_bank.short_answer: Ask dLocal to sign an approval-rate SLA in writing. They won't. Yuno will.
objection_bank.full_response: I can't prove it to you in the abstract and neither can they. So here's the test I'd run: ask dLocal's sales team one question in writing — 'Will you commit to matching or exceeding our current January 2025 blended card approval rate of 79.5%, with penalty clauses in the contract?' They won't sign it. Yuno will. Multi-acquirer orchestration is the only architecture that can make that commitment. That's not a marketing claim, it's a structural fact — dLocal has one acquirer in Peru, and they can't route around a decline they don't see. The SLA is the proof.

objection_bank.objection: If you're so good, why did TPV drop 8% in January?
objection_bank.short_answer: It's seasonal. Proportional across all 4 markets. Support tickets stayed flat.
objection_bank.full_response: Fair question, and it's the first thing I'd ask too. The answer is: it's seasonal. Three data points. One — LatAm BNPL always drops 5 to 12 percent in January after the December peak. PagBrasil, Mercado Pago, dLocal's own public data all show this pattern. Two — the decline is proportional: Mexico down 8.5, Colombia down 8.0, Chile down 8.7, Peru down 8.3. If merchants were churning, we'd see asymmetric drops. Three — support tickets went from 12 to 10, down slightly. A platform problem shows up in tickets. Your 6-month TPV growth is still +54%. The dip is mean reversion, not a structural problem.

objection_bank.objection: We need named support and a Slack channel with engineering. Yuno hasn't delivered that.
objection_bank.short_answer: You requested it 5 times. That's a Yuno failure. Fixing it by end of week.
objection_bank.full_response: This one I own directly. You've requested a dedicated Slack channel with Yuno engineering five times in the last six months, and we haven't delivered. That's not a dLocal advantage, it's a Yuno service failure. Here's what happens this week — regardless of the renewal outcome: dedicated Slack channel live by Friday, named CSM assigned with weekly (not bi-weekly) syncs, direct escalation path to our Solutions Engineering team, and I'll be your primary point of contact with 4-hour response SLA. I should have had this in place 5 months ago. I'm fixing it now.

objection_bank.objection: What happens to our integration if Yuno has an outage?
objection_bank.short_answer: Multi-acquirer fallback is exactly why orchestration exists. Single-acquirer platforms can't say that.
objection_bank.full_response: This is actually the strongest structural argument for Yuno vs dLocal. A Yuno outage is rare, but when an acquirer has an outage — which happens more often — Yuno's multi-acquirer routing automatically fails over to another acquirer in real time. On a single-acquirer platform, when your acquirer goes down, your payments go down. Period. We can show you the reliability numbers: in the last 18 months, FlexiPay has seen zero full-platform outages on Yuno, but Yuno's multi-acquirer routing caught and rerouted on 4 separate acquirer incidents — each one of which would have been a measurable revenue event on a single-pipe setup. That's not a cost. That's insurance you're already paying for.

objection_bank.objection: Switching vendors isn't that hard. dLocal says 6-8 weeks.
objection_bank.short_answer: Their 6-8 weeks. Your 160 engineering hours. Plus Brazil delay. Plus 1,280 opportunity hours.
objection_bank.full_response: dLocal's 6-8 weeks is their onboarding timeline, not your engineering effort. The real cost is on your side: at a minimum, 160 engineering hours for re-integration, token migration, webhook rewiring, reconciliation rebuild, and production validation. That's 4 weeks of 4 engineers, and you have 4 engineers total. The opportunity cost — the stuff those engineers are NOT shipping during those 4 weeks — is 1,280 engineering-hours. Those are the same hours that should be shipping the Brazil launch Sofia committed to the board. So switching doesn't cost you 6-8 weeks. It costs you Brazil Q1. And at Base case that's $337K of lost FlexiPay revenue in the first two months alone.

objection_bank.objection: dLocal is a public company. They're more stable than Yuno.
objection_bank.short_answer: Stability isn't the question. Speed to Brazil is. dLocal adds 6 weeks of delay.
objection_bank.full_response: I understand why that matters — as a Series B company, vendor stability is a real concern. But the question isn't which vendor is more stable. The question is which vendor lets you ship Brazil in Q1. Yuno supports PIX, Boleto, and Brazilian local acquiring on your existing integration — zero new code, 3 weeks to live. dLocal requires full re-integration, 6 to 8 weeks minimum. You told the board 2025 is your Brazil year. Which vendor helps you make good on that commitment? And on stability — Yuno has processed over $107M of your TPV over the last 6 months with zero critical outages. The track record is in your reporting.

objection_bank.objection: If we sign with dLocal now, we lock in pricing for 24 months. That's stability.
objection_bank.short_answer: It's a $345K/year trap. Your Jan TPV is $20.7M. Their floor is $25M.
objection_bank.full_response: 24 months at their price is 24 months of a $4.3M per month TPV shortfall against their minimum. Their contract includes a $25M/month TPV floor — your January was $20.7M. If they enforce committed volume billing — which is standard in LatAm PSP contracts — you pay for $25M whether you process it or not. At 0.67%, that's $345,720 per year of overage exposure, on top of the $18 basis points you thought you were saving. And that's just the billing risk. If you miss the minimum for three consecutive months, most of these contracts have repricing or early termination clauses. It's not stability — it's a trap with a cheaper headline rate.

objection_bank.objection: Why should I care about payments infrastructure? It's plumbing.
objection_bank.short_answer: Because plumbing is what ships Brazil in 3 weeks instead of 8.
objection_bank.full_response: Sofia, I'd agree with you — it is plumbing, and plumbing shouldn't be a strategic conversation. But in your specific situation, right now, this plumbing decision determines whether you ship Brazil in Q1. Yuno's existing integration supports PIX and Brazilian local acquiring today. Switching resets that clock by 6 to 8 weeks minimum. That's 6 to 8 weeks of first-mover advantage you cannot get back in a market where your competitors are also racing. Under our Base case, Brazil and Argentina generate $1.24M of net FlexiPay revenue in Year 1 — 2.9 times the dLocal headline savings. The plumbing decision is the Brazil decision.

objection_bank.objection: I want to stay with Yuno but Carlos won't let me. What do I do?
objection_bank.short_answer: Co-present the QBR with me. Hand Carlos the switching-cost math yourself.
objection_bank.full_response: Here's what works: don't make it a Daniela vs Carlos conversation. Make it a Daniela + Nicolas vs the numbers conversation. I want you to co-present the QBR tomorrow with me. I hand you the math, you hand Carlos the math. That way it's not your opinion versus his mandate — it's joint analysis. I've prepared specific talking points for each of his likely objections, with the exact numbers. Second: I'm requesting a call between Yuno's CEO and Sofia this week. If Sofia hears the strategic framing from a peer, it reframes the conversation from 'vendor line item' to 'strategic partnership for the Series B expansion'. Third: I'll deliver a written counter-proposal to Carlos tomorrow that addresses every dLocal point on paper. He can't argue with math he has in front of him. You don't carry this alone.

objection_bank.objection: I need to think about it and talk to the board.
objection_bank.short_answer: Take the week. But sign the Brazil fast-track letter of intent today.
objection_bank.full_response: Of course — this is a board-level decision and I'd expect you to take it to them. What I'd ask is that we decouple two decisions. The renewal decision can wait a week — I trust your process. But the Brazil fast-track decision can't wait, because every week we delay loses you first-mover advantage and ships $2.5M of deferred Base-case TPV into February. So here's what I propose: you take the week on the renewal. In parallel, we sign a Letter of Intent today that locks Yuno as your Brazil launch partner and starts the Solutions Engineering work at no cost to you. If the renewal goes your way, the Brazil work is already underway and you're live in 3 weeks. If the renewal doesn't go our way — which I don't think will happen — the LOI is non-binding and you've lost nothing. What's the downside?

objection_bank.short_answer: Platform fee waived. PIX at 0.50%. 18-month term. Approval SLA. BR fast-track. $75K total give.
objection_bank.full_response: Final offer, in writing by end of day: one — platform fee waived, saves you $24,000 per year. Two — PIX take rate matched to dLocal at 0.50% on a 24-month Brazil commitment. Three — 18-month term, no hard TPV minimum, pricing review only if TPV drops more than 25% for 3 consecutive months. Four — performance SLA with market-specific approval-rate floors and service credits if we miss. Five — 40 hours of Solutions Engineering dedicated to your Brazil fast-track, zero cost to you. Six — dedicated CSM and Slack channel live this week. Total value of concessions: approximately $75,000 per year. In exchange, Yuno keeps the card take rate at 0.85% — which is where the performance guarantee lives — and you get Brazil live in 3 weeks, Argentina ready to follow, and an account team accountable to outcomes. That's the offer. Do we have a deal?

pricing_floor.note: Cards at 0.78% is the absolute floor per VP. Below this resets LatAm pricing for all accounts.

situation.executive_brief: FlexiPay is an 18-month Yuno account in high-risk renewal with three overlapping pressures: a CFO-driven cost reduction mandate, a champion who needs ammunition, and a Brazil expansion window that closes in Q1. dLocal has submitted a competitor proposal designed to look cheaper on paper by $427K/yr, but which destroys $707K of FlexiPay value once switching costs are priced in. Our job is to surface the iceberg, empower the champion, and convert a defensive renewal conversation into a strategic partnership decision framed around Brazil speed — where Yuno structurally wins.

four_forces.name: Cost Pressure
four_forces.data: 6 months in seat with board mandate to extend runway post-Series A
four_forces.pull: Reduce vendor spend, measurable line-item savings
four_forces.tension: Wants visible cost wins, even at strategic cost

four_forces.name: Performance Expectation
four_forces.data: NPS 7 passive · verbatim mentioned PE routing inconsistency + support slippage
four_forces.pull: Approval rate uplift, faster expansion support, dedicated ops
four_forces.tension: Trusts Yuno but needs quantified proof for CFO

four_forces.name: Brazil Urgency
four_forces.data: $50M Series B closed Jan 15, 2025 · public commitment to 2025 Brazil
four_forces.pull: First-mover advantage before competitors lock up market
four_forces.tension: Cares about speed, not vendor line items

four_forces.name: Engineering Constraint
four_forces.data: 4 backend devs · 12 open roles · 2,000 merchant partners to manage
four_forces.pull: Minimize burden of any payments work · protect dev capacity
four_forces.tension: Switching consumes 1,280 hours that should ship Brazil

meddpicc.label: Metrics
meddpicc.filled: Approval rate uplift (current 79.5% → target 82%), Brazil TPV Base case $11M/mo M6, $305K/yr approval gap upside, $1.24M FlexiPay Y1 net revenue upside under Base case expansion.

meddpicc.label: Economic Buyer
meddpicc.filled: Carlos Mendoza — CFO, 6 months in seat, post-Series A cost-cutting mandate. Approves or rejects commercial terms. Cold relationship. Focus: ROI and margin expansion, not partnership narrative.

meddpicc.label: Decision Criteria
meddpicc.filled: Cost (weight ~40%), payment performance SLA (~30%), Brazil go-live speed (~20%), engineering effort (~10%). Our proposal wins on 3 of 4 — we lose on headline cost until switching costs are priced in.

meddpicc.label: Decision Process
meddpicc.filled: Daniela recommends → Carlos approves commercial terms → Sofia breaks ties on strategic direction. 60-day notice clause in current contract. If no notice given, contract auto-renews on March 15, 2025.

meddpicc.label: Paper Process
meddpicc.filled: Standard SaaS renewal — legal review by both sides, procurement sign-off at FlexiPay, ~2-week cycle. No SOC2 or security review blockers (already cleared for initial contract).

meddpicc.label: Identify Pain
meddpicc.filled: Peru routing at 72.7% approval (−7.3pt vs 80% benchmark). Colombia at 75.7% (−4.3pt). Dedicated support channel requested 5 times, not delivered. Brazil unlaunched despite Series B mandate. 1,280 eng-hours needed for any migration = Brazil delay.

meddpicc.label: Champion
meddpicc.filled: Daniela Ramos — Head of Payments, ex-Mercado Pago. Strong trust. Owes Yuno for 2 past delivery wins (CO acquirer in 3 days, webhook debug in 24h). Data-driven, hates fluff. Quote: 'If we're really better, prove it with numbers.'

meddpicc.label: Competition
meddpicc.filled: dLocal — public company, primarily cross-border PSP and local acquirer. Single-acquirer architecture in PE/CO. 24-month contract, $25M/mo TPV minimum ($4.3M overage for FlexiPay). Offer: 0.67% cards, $0 platform fee, 20h free BR integration, 24-mo lock-in.

stakeholder_matrix.name: Daniela Ramos
stakeholder_matrix.tactic: Hand her the math. Co-present the QBR. Make it her win, not yours.

stakeholder_matrix.name: Carlos Mendoza
stakeholder_matrix.tactic: Bottom-line first. Correct his numbers ($370K → $427K). Show the iceberg. Give him a visible win ($75K concession package).

stakeholder_matrix.name: Sofia Alvarez
stakeholder_matrix.tactic: Elevate the conversation to strategic. Brazil first-mover framing. Yuno CEO → Sofia call. Her lens is market capture, not line items.

value_pyramid.yuno_value: Enables Sofia's 2025 Brazil mandate in Q1 — the #1 public commitment to her board. Protects first-mover advantage in a time-sensitive market.
value_pyramid.dlocal_value: None. dLocal delays Brazil by 6-8 weeks, forfeiting Sofia's Q1 window.

value_pyramid.yuno_value: Reduces risk (proven 18-month reliability, zero outages). Reduces effort (no new integration for Brazil). Protects FlexiPay engineering team from switching burden.
value_pyramid.dlocal_value: 1,280 hours of engineering diverted to re-integration. Tokenization migration risk. Cross-vendor transition stress.

value_pyramid.yuno_value: Processes payments. Current ARR to FlexiPay $2.06M. Median LatAm pricing (0.83% effective).
value_pyramid.dlocal_value: Processes payments. Headline 0.67% cards. $427K/yr cheaper on paper. This is the only level where dLocal competes.
value_pyramid.note: dLocal only competes at Level 1. Yuno wins Levels 2 and 3 structurally. The renewal conversation has to move up the pyramid.

key_principles.explanation: Dropping the card rate forfeits the performance SLA — which is the single biggest structural differentiator vs dLocal. Below 0.80% also sets a LatAm pricing floor for every other Yuno account. Discipline over easy wins.

FlexiPay's 4-dev team is their binding constraint, not their budget. Every decision should minimize engineering burden. Brazil fast-track on existing Yuno instance = 0 new integration, while dLocal costs them 1,280 eng-hours.
Sofia's priority is first-mover advantage, not pricing. Yuno gets FlexiPay live in 3 weeks vs dLocal's 6-8 weeks. That 5-week delta is worth more than the $427K headline savings.

Every competitive proposal looks good on headline. The KAM's job is to surface the hidden structural costs — switching effort, opportunity cost, performance risk, commitment overage — so the buyer sees the full cost, not the brochure.

A KAM Leader operates with three audiences simultaneously: the client (FlexiPay), internal leadership (Yuno VP), and the champion (Daniela). Each needs a different artifact with the same underlying numbers. One backend, three outputs, zero copy-paste.

risk_register.risk: Carlos overrules Daniela despite quantified case
risk_register.mitigation: Yuno CEO → Sofia escalation call within 14 days. Reframe from vendor line item to strategic partnership. Offer written counter with iceberg visual.

risk_register.risk: Approval-rate sprint misses targets in Peru
risk_register.mitigation: Pre-negotiate Niubiz or Izipay as 3rd PE acquirer within 30 days. Market-specific SLA floors: PE at 75% (not 80%). Rolling 90-day measurement.

risk_register.risk: FlexiPay delays renewal decision past 45 days
risk_register.mitigation: Auto-renew clause works in our favor. Schedule 'Brazil readiness review' on day 30 to force decision. Decouple renewal from Brazil LOI.

risk_register.risk: dLocal drops card take rate below 0.60%
risk_register.mitigation: Do NOT engage in price war. Pivot to outcome-based terms: performance SLA with service credits, rebate per approval point above floor. Hold 0.80% as absolute floor.

Broaden champion base to FlexiPay engineering (4 devs). Document all Yuno deliverables in writing so successor has evidence without her endorsement. Offer her Yuno advisory role within 48h if she leaves.
6-month safety-net clause for win-back. Turn FlexiPay's 18-mo track record into public case study. Extract post-mortem data from Daniela for pipeline use.

january_dip_analysis.headline: January -8.4% is seasonal, not structural
evidence: Proportional decline across all 4 markets: MX -8.5%, CO -8.0%, CL -8.7%, PE -8.3%
Support tickets flat (12 → 10) — a platform problem would spike tickets
LatAm BNPL seasonality: Dec peak is anomaly, Jan is reversion — PagBrasil, Mercado Pago, dLocal public data confirm

payment_method_efficiency.insight: Best-performing local method. No action needed — protect this share.
Slight optimization opportunity. Focus on cards, which are the drag.

support_ticket_analysis.headline: Support ticket volume is a warning signal we need to fix — it's NOT a reason to leave
support_ticket_analysis.insight: 71% increase from Aug to Dec. Dominant topic: routing (5 tickets in Dec, 4 in Jan). This confirms the approval-rate sprint is the right intervention. Routing tickets = FlexiPay's own engineers noticing the problem we should have fixed already.

nps_verbatim.verbatim: Yuno saves us a ton of integration work, but routing performance in Peru is inconsistent and support response times have slipped.

decoded.they_said: Yuno saves us a ton of integration work
decoded.what_that_means: They know the switching cost. They don't want to do it again. This is an advantage we haven't fully leveraged — make it explicit in the QBR.

decoded.they_said: routing performance in Peru is inconsistent
decoded.what_that_means: Peru routing is a concrete, quantified gap (−7.3pt). It's the first thing I'd fix. 90-day sprint commitment with SLA floor.
This is operational debt Yuno owes FlexiPay. Fix it this week — dedicated Slack, named CSM, 4h SLA — regardless of renewal outcome. It's the right thing to do.

cost_efficiency.analysis: FlexiPay is paying the LatAm orchestration median. dLocal's 0.67% is bottom-of-market, which is single-acquirer pricing territory — a different product, not a better deal.
